---
title: "Portrait scientific poster example"
format:
  poster-typst:
    size: "36x48"
    poster-authors: "First Author¹, Second Author², Senior Author¹"
    departments: "¹ Department or Group, University · ² Collaborating Department, Institution"
    institution-logo: "../figures/logo-placeholder.svg"
    footer-text: "Conference Name · City, Country · Year"
    footer-url: "https://example.org/poster"
    footer-emails: "first.author@example.edu"
    footer-color: "003057"
    keywords: ["portrait", "quarto", "poster"]
    num-columns: 2
    title-font-size: 50
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

# Motivation

Portrait posters often work well when the conference board is tall or when the story needs a strong top-to-bottom flow.

# Approach

Use two body columns for a portrait poster unless the content is extremely compact.

- Keep text blocks short.
- Use large figures.
- Put the most important result near the upper half.

# Example result

![Example result figure.](../figures/example-results.svg){width="100%"}

# Main message

::: {.block fill="rgb(232, 241, 250)" inset="12pt" radius="6pt"}

Replace this callout with the single most important scientific result.

:::

# Conclusions

- Keep the main conclusions brief.
- Link to details with a QR code or short URL.
- Check the PDF page size before printing.

# Contact

![QR-code placeholder.](../figures/qr-placeholder.svg){width="30%"}

<https://example.org/poster>
